search_keywords.description: Comma-separated domain-specific keywords for routing evidence to this section. Include technical terms, units, method names. E.g. 'epoxy, MPa, dolly, ASTM D4541, cohesive failure'
search_keywords.title: Search Keywords

evidence_summary.description: Summary of evidence from conversations for this section
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives that contributed to this section
perspectives.title: Perspectives

sections.description: Ordered sections for the research report
sections.title: Sections

schema_json.title: StormOutlinePlan

prompt:
You are a research outline architect. Based on multi-perspective expert interviews, create a comprehensive report outline.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

INTERVIEW CONVERSATIONS (5 perspectives, 15 total Q&A rounds):

=== Scientific (Dr. Hiroshi Tanaka) ===
  Q: Across the most rigorous independent systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials published 2023-2025, what are the pooled point estimates (SMD/WMD with 95% confidence interval
  A: The 2023-2025 meta-analytic literature recovered here converges on significant IF benefit across multiple metabolic-syndrome-relevant endpoints, though the independent pooled point estimates vary by population mix (MetS-specific vs cardiometabolic vs T2DM-inclusive) and by protocol heterogeneity. The MetS-specific Song et al. 2025 Frontiers in Nutrition systematic review and meta-analysis with GRADE evaluation (Sources 1, 2, 3, 6) — the work co-authored by Song, Almutairi, and colleagues and ind
  Key findings: Song et al. 2025 (Frontiers in Nutrition, Sources 1, 2, 3, 6) is the flagship MetS-specific systemat; The 8-RCT / 573-participant MetS meta-analysis (PMC12363089, Source 5) in the Journal of Health, Pop; The T2DM-inclusive PubMed meta-analysis (PMID 40367729, Source 15) reports SMD = -0.93 (95% CI: -1.6; The 16/8 TRE meta-analysis in Nutrition Reviews (Source 4) documents improvements in fasting glucose; Information gap: none of the search snippets provide the endpoint-by-endpoint GRADE certainty letter
  Q: Round 1 answers surfaced that MetS-specific meta-analyses converge on direction but not on comparable point estimates, and that heterogeneity (I² up to 93.8%) is a dominant feature of the IF RCT liter
  A: The search results confirm that meta-regression and subgroup analyses have identified several consistent moderators of the high heterogeneity in IF RCT outcomes, but no single snippet provides a complete I2-resolution table across the MetS endpoint panel. The Wiley Obesity Reviews paper by Shen et al. (Source 3, onlinelibrary doi:10.1111/obr.13855) on longer-term IF effects on body composition and cardiometabolic outcomes states explicitly that 'subgroup analyses showed that the mode of IF and i
  Key findings: Shen et al. 2024 Obesity Reviews (Source 3): the mode of IF and intervention duration are identified; Nutrition Reviews 16/8 TRE meta-analysis (Source 5): participant sex, physical activity level, and i; Nature IJO 2024 TRE+exercise meta-analysis (Source 7): BMI, exercise type, intervention duration, an; MedRxiv early/midday/late TRE subgroup analysis (Source 8): shorter intervention durations are assoc; No snippet reports the specific I2 reduction after moderator adjustment for any endpoint in the MetS
  Q: Round 2 established that protocol type, intervention duration, sex, PA, and BMI are the dominant moderators of IF effect heterogeneity, but no snippet reported the specific I² reduction after adjustme
  A: The search results provide a partial answer on both methodological concerns. On publication bias in the IF literature: Source 2 (MDPI Nutrients TRE fat-loss review) reports 'the funnel plot and Egger's test (t = 0.35, p = 0.731) indicated no publication bias' for TRE effects on fat loss, with subgroup analyses showing 6-8 hour eating windows are more effective. However, Source 3 (the ResearchGate funnel-plot figure) reports 'Egger's regression test revealed potential evidence of publication bias
  Key findings: MDPI Nutrients TRE fat-loss review (Source 2) reports Egger's test t = 0.35, p = 0.731 — NO evidence; A second funnel-plot analysis (Source 3, ResearchGate) reports Egger's test t = -5.4, p < 0.001 for ; Source 11 and 14 (Ulisboa/PMC Ramadan meta-analysis) explicitly state non-Ramadan IF is MORE effecti; Source 13 (Longo 2020 Obesity review) notes meta-analyses of Ramadan fasting show benefits among hea; Information gap: no source provides head-to-head quantitative reconciliation of RCT vs Ramadan SMD v

=== Regulatory (Maria Escalante, JD) ===
  Q: What formal regulatory opinions, guidance documents, or enforcement actions have the FDA, EMA, EFSA, or Health Canada issued regarding intermittent fasting as a therapeutic or preventive intervention 
  A: The search results reveal a regulatory landscape in which no authority — FDA, EMA, EFSA, or Health Canada — has issued a formal therapeutic opinion on intermittent fasting for metabolic syndrome. The FDA 2024 activity (Sources 1-10) has focused on adjacent instruments: a draft guidance on Dietary Guidance Statements in food labeling (December 2024), the final rule updating the voluntary 'healthy' nutrient content claim (December 19, 2024; 89 FR 106064), and a Front-of-Package Nutrition Informati
  Key findings: FDA issued a draft guidance on Dietary Guidance Statements in 2024 (Sources 1, 2, 5) but it does not; FDA finalized an updated 'healthy' nutrient content claim rule on December 19, 2024 (Sources 3, 4, 7; EFSA evaluates health claims under Article 13 of Regulation 1924/2006 (Source 11), and the Panel on ; The EFSA Article 13 consolidated list contains 4,185 main health claim entries (Source 12) but the s; No warning letters, enforcement actions, or formal therapeutic opinions from FDA, EMA, EFSA, or Heal
  Q: Round 1 established that no authority has issued a formal therapeutic opinion on intermittent fasting, yet commercial IF apps (Zero, Fastic, Simple, LIFE Fasting Tracker) market themselves to users wi
  A: The search results reveal a stark asymmetry between the volume and specificity of IF app marketing claims and the complete absence of direct FTC or FDA enforcement actions against them. The leading commercial app Zero (owned by Big Sky Health, founded by Kevin Rose) explicitly advertises 'unlock the full benefits of intermittent fasting to lose weight and burn fat faster' on Google Play (Source 1) and markets itself as 'the all-in-one fasting tracker and nutrition app built for real results' on 
  Key findings: Zero (Big Sky Health / Kevin Rose) explicitly markets weight-loss and fat-burn claims on Google Play; FTC enforcement on health claims is active but targeted at dietary supplements: March 2022 order aga; The regulatory framework for substantiation exists: CRS R48623 (Source 14) explains FDA qualified he; No FTC, FDA, ASA, or MHRA enforcement action named in the search results targets an intermittent fas; Information gap: the search snippets do not recover Fastic, Simple, or LIFE Fasting Tracker specific
  Q: Rounds 1-2 established that (a) no regulator has issued a formal IF therapeutic opinion, and (b) commercial IF apps market weight-loss and fat-burn benefits without facing enforcement. Now consider th
  A: The search results address FTC substantiation standards comprehensively but do not recover a direct precedent of either agency acting on a conference abstract alone. On FTC substantiation: Source 1 (FTC Policy Statement Regarding Advertising Substantiation) and Source 5 (FTC Advertising Substantiation Principles PDF) codify the 'reasonable basis doctrine' — firms must have substantiation BEFORE disseminating a claim, and an advertiser must substantiate all express and implied claims. Source 10 (
  Key findings: FTC substantiation controlling standard (Source 10, Nutraceuticals World): 'competent and reliable s; FTC Policy Statement (Source 1) and Advertising Substantiation Principles (Source 5) codify the reas; The 2022 FTC Health Products Compliance Guidance (Source 6, MoFo) is the first update in nearly 25 y; FDA's postmarketing safety surveillance framework (Source 13) takes a multidisciplinary, life-cycle ; Bidirectional exclusion: the same substantiation standard that would EXCLUDE a non-peer-reviewed con

=== Industry (Rajiv Menon, MBA) ===
  Q: What do real-world usage, retention, and adherence data from commercial intermittent-fasting tracking apps (Zero, Fastic, Simple, LIFE Fasting Tracker, DoFasting, Window, Lasta) tell us about how the 
  A: The search results recover one large-scale published real-world study on IF app retention and outcomes, plus numerous marketing and company-facing pages. Source 1 and Source 4 (JMIR / ResearchGate preprint) report a 52-week observational study of 2 IF mobile apps quantifying user retention, fasting patterns, and weight loss — this is the direct academic evidence base. Source 4 specifically notes 'By week 26, users lost over 1% of their starting weight per EFH per day on average' (EFH = eligible 
  Key findings: JMIR / PMC9579929 52-week observational study of 2 IF apps (Sources 1, 4): users lost over 1% of sta; SIMPLE app real-world study (Source 6, PMC11091450): demonstrates weight-loss effectiveness in adult; Alibaba product-insights commentary (Source 8) quotes concrete Zero retention: 57% at day 30 and 39%; Zero markets itself as the 'world's most popular intermittent fasting app' (Source 3, Google Play) a; Information gap: no source recovers (a) subscription churn tied to the AHA 2024 CV-mortality news cy
  Q: Round 1 identified the JMIR 52-week observational study of 2 IF apps as the main real-world evidence source and reported 1% weight loss per eligible fasting hour per day by week 26. What were the spec
  A: The search results confirm and expand on the JMIR 52-week observational study of IF mobile apps. Source 9 (ResearchGate preprint 357413255) discloses the most important detail: 'Intermittent Fasting with the LIFE mobile apps appears to be a sustainable approach to weight reduction' — establishing that the studied apps are the LIFE family, not Zero. Sources 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 are the published JMIR mHealth uHealth 2022 paper (Torres et al. / JMIR 10(10):e35896, PMID 36194463). The most concrete 
  Key findings: The JMIR 52-week study focused on the LIFE family of IF mobile apps (Source 9, ResearchGate 35741325; BMI-stratified weight loss (Sources 1, 4, mhealth.jmir.org/2022/10/e35896, PMID 36194463): users wit; Retention operationalized as recorded-fasts activity (Sources 3, 11), not subscription renewal — an ; The concrete weight-loss stratification counters generic IF marketing: the app population benefits m; Information gap: sample size N, week-by-week retention curve, protocol breakdown (16:8 vs 5:2 vs ADF
  Q: Round 2 revealed that the JMIR 52-week LIFE app study showed 13.9% weight loss in BMI≥40 users but slight weight GAIN in lower-BMI users. Two industry questions follow: (1) What self-reported adverse 
  A: The search results recover a substantive safety-signals literature on eating-disorder risk but NO IF-app-specific post-market surveillance data or adjustment of marketing based on BMI stratification. On eating-disorder risk: Source 2 (PMC10589984 editorial in BMC Clinical Diabetes and Endocrinology) discusses 'the risks of disordered eating' with IF, citing a comprehensive review by Albosta et al. Source 3 (The Emily Program blog) explicitly states 'Intermittent fasting is a restrictive behavior
  Key findings: Eating-disorder clinical community has explicitly flagged IF as a concerning restrictive behavior ov; PMC10589984 (Source 2, BMC Clinical Diabetes and Endocrinology editorial) cites Albosta et al. compr; Hypoglycemia is the documented acute adverse-event mechanism for IF, particularly in Ramadan cohorts; Commercial IF apps (Zero, Fastic) continue to market uniformly to all users without BMI stratificati; Information gap: no source recovers IF-app-specific post-market surveillance data, FDA MAUDE reports

=== Economic (Dr. Eleanor Whitfield) ===
  Q: What is the evidence base on the cost-effectiveness of intermittent fasting as an intervention for metabolic syndrome — specifically, have any published economic evaluations quantified the incremental
  A: The search results recover ONE published IF-specific economic evaluation plus several adjacent references. Sources 1, 3, and 5 (PMC12599020, ResearchGate, PubMed 41214519) all describe the same study: a trial-based analysis and lifetime model-based analysis of the cost-effectiveness of a one-year monthly 5-consecutive-day fasting-mimicking diet (FMD) programme in patients with type 2 diabetes. Source 2 (ScienceDirect) confirms this is described as 'the first [study] to evaluate the costs and con
  Key findings: Peer-reviewed IF economic evaluation is essentially ONE study (Sources 1, 2, 3, 5): a trial-based an; No ICER per QALY, budget impact, or cost-offset value is disclosed in the search snippets for the FM; The ICER institute type 2 diabetes report (Sources 4, 9, 10) establishes the methodological benchmar; The PMC5695352 systematic review (Source 11) finds lifestyle programmes and metformin are cost-effec; Critical gap: the economic evidence base for IF (TRE, ADF, 5:2, ICR) in metabolic syndrome is essent
  Q: Round 1 identified that the only published IF-specific economic evaluation is the trial-based and lifetime model-based cost-utility analysis of a monthly 5-day fasting-mimicking diet (FMD) programme i
  A: The search results provide partial specifics on the FMD-in-T2D cost-utility study. Source 2 (ScienceDirect, doi S1098301524028468) and Source 9 (PubMed 39343090) — the same paper with two links — disclose the key effectiveness claims: the FMD programme was associated with '11.4% less diabetes complications, 67.2% less overall diabetes medication use' versus standard care in the cost-utility analysis. These two effect magnitudes are the closest the snippets get to ICER inputs. Source 14 (Semantic
  Key findings: Burg-Peet FMD-in-T2D cost-utility analysis (Sources 2, 9): FMD was associated with 11.4% fewer diabe; Semantic Scholar summary (Source 14) explicitly states: 'The FMD programme does not appear to be cos; Dutch cost-utility convention used (Source 15, FIT trial thesis): 4% discount on costs, 1.5% discoun; Source 10 (ResearchGate 384414696) describes a separate US-setting cost-utility analysis of FMD vs s; Information gap: specific ICER per QALY, net monetary benefit, incremental cost components, and prob
  Q: Round 2 established the Burg-Peet FMD-in-T2D study is NOT cost-effective over the first year in the Dutch healthcare system. This raises a methodological concern: given that IF interventions are essen
  A: The search results provide direct Prolon/L-Nutra branded FMD pricing plus several generic lifestyle intervention cost-effectiveness references. On Prolon cost: Sources 1, 3, 6, 9, and 10 all disclose the Prolon 5-Day Fasting-Mimicking Program retail prices: Source 3 lists the SKU ladder — Original $205 (discounted $185), Gen3 $205 ($185), Expanded Variety $215 ($195), Next Gen $255 ($215) per 5-day kit; Source 6 (Prolon's own marketing) says 'One round of the Prolon 5-Day FMD costs between $175 
  Key findings: Prolon 5-Day FMD kit retail price is approximately $175-$255 per 5-day kit (Sources 3, 6, 7, 9) — ro; The Burg-Peet one-year monthly FMD protocol implies ~12 × $185 = $2,220 per patient per year in bran; Generic lifestyle interventions (DPP, Indian trial, Chinese modeling) are typically cost-effective o; Physical activity interventions appear cost-saving; diet and diet+PA interventions are likely to be ; Unmet need: no source directly models an unbranded TRE or 5:2 cost-effectiveness, meaning the fastin

=== Public_Health (Dr. Amara Okonkwo, MPH) ===
  Q: What does the population-level observational evidence — including the March 2024 American Heart Association EPI/Lifestyle conference abstract by Zhong/Chen et al. (NHANES 2003-2018 with ~20,078 U.S. a
  A: The population-level observational evidence on IF cardiovascular safety comprises two distinct and partially contradictory bodies of evidence. The first is the March 2024 Zhong/Chen et al. AHA EPI/Lifestyle conference abstract (P192), now partially peer-reviewed. Source 3 (PubMed 40849219) is the peer-reviewed journal publication of the analysis, which concludes: 'Although a positive association was observed between eating duration <8 h and cardiovascular mortality, further research is required 
  Key findings: The Zhong/Chen NHANES 2003-2018 analysis has been peer-reviewed (Source 3, PubMed 40849219) and stil; Methodological critique (Source 6, Orthomolecular News): the NHANES 2003-2018 dietary questionnaires; Ramadan observational literature (Sources 11-15) consistently shows NO INCREASE in cardiovascular ev; PMC4859102 (Source 12) provides direct null evidence: CV event incidence during Ramadan is similar t; The prudent public-health position: the observational literatures disagree, the NHANES signal is sus
  Q: Round 1 established that the NHANES short-eating-window signal and the Ramadan observational literature disagree on long-term cardiovascular risk, with reverse causation as a likely reconciliation. Sh
  A: The search results reveal a stark evidence asymmetry: substantial LMIC-focused epidemiology of metabolic syndrome exists, but very little LMIC-specific RCT evidence on IF is available. On sub-Saharan Africa: Source 1 (PMC1961628) and Source 4 (ResearchGate 5562784) assess MetS prevalence and aetiology in sub-Saharan Africa using multiple definitions, confirming MetS is a significant burden in the region but NOT reporting any IF intervention studies. No IF RCT drawn from a sub-Saharan African pop
  Key findings: Substantial MetS epidemiology exists for sub-Saharan Africa (Sources 1, 4, PMC1961628) but NO sub-Sa; South Asian populations have elevated cardiometabolic risk at lower BMI than Western thresholds pred; Ramadan fasting is the main LMIC-relevant IF evidence base, with Source 11 (PMC12274119) examining R; NO Latin American, Mexican, or Brazilian IF trial appears in the recovered results — a complete evid; Expected effect-size modifiers: lower BMI thresholds in South Asians, food-insecurity interaction in
  Q: Round 2 identified a stark evidence gap: no sub-Saharan African or Latin American IF RCTs exist, and the South Asian evidence is limited to Ramadan studies and diaspora populations. From a population-
  A: The search results provide substantive evidence on each of the harm vectors raised in the question, especially on micronutrient inadequacy, adolescent risk, and the food-insecurity / disordered-eating linkage. On micronutrient compromise: Source 1 (PubMed 40657741) states explicitly: 'Although intermittent fasting is an effective and flexible approach to weight management, it may compromise micronutrient intake if dietary [quality is not maintained]' — a direct peer-reviewed finding that IF redu
  Key findings: PubMed 40657741 (Source 1): IF 'may compromise micronutrient intake if dietary quality is not mainta; PMC11247931 Scoping Review of IF in Youth (Source 3): intermittent fasting is linked to INCREASED di; U of Minnesota cohort (Source 5): household food insecurity during adolescence is related to disorde; F1000Research Malawi/Ghana (Source 10): LMIC adolescent populations already face limited dietary div; PLoS Medicine Lifestyle Africa cluster RCT (Source 12): DPP-style community lifestyle interventions 

PERSPECTIVES COVERED: Economic, Industry, Regulatory, Scientific, Public_Health

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Identify 8-15 thematic clusters across ALL conversations.
2. For each cluster, create a report section with:
   - title: Clear, descriptive section heading
   - description: 2-3 sentences on what this section should cover
   - evidence_summary: Key findings from conversations that belong here
   - perspectives: Which STORM perspectives contributed to this section
   - order: Sequential position (1-based)
3. Sections should be ordered logically: background -> methodology -> findings -> analysis -> implications -> future directions.
4. Each section should draw from at least 2 different perspectives where possible (multi-perspective synthesis).
5. Do NOT create a section for every single Q&A pair — cluster related information together.
6. Ensure COMPLETE COVERAGE: every key finding from the interviews should appear in at least one section's evidence_summary.

Return a JSON object with a 'sections' array of outline section objects.
